records.ety: [F. prjudice, L. praejudicium; prae before + judicium judgment. See Prejudicate, Judicial.]

quotes: Naught might hinder his quick prejudize.

defs: An opinion or judgment formed without due examination; prejudgment; a leaning toward one side of a question from other considerations than those belonging to it; an unreasonable predilection for, or objection against, anything; especially, an opinion or leaning adverse to anything, without just grounds, or before sufficient knowledge.
quotes: Though often misled by prejudice and passion, he was emphatically an honest man.

defs: A bias on the part of judge, juror, or witness which interferes with fairness of judgment.

quotes: England and France might, through their amity, Breed him some prejudice.

records.morph: [imp. & p. p. Prejudiced (?); p. pr. & vb. n. Prejudicing (?).]

defs: To cause to have prejudice; to prepossess with opinions formed without due knowledge or examination; to bias the mind of, by hasty and incorrect notions; to give an unreasonable bent to, as to one side or the other of a cause; as, to prejudice a critic or a juryman.
quotes: Suffer not any beloved study to prejudice your mind so far as to despise all other learning.

defs: To obstruct or injure by prejudices, or by previous bias of the mind; hence, generally, to hurt; to damage; to injure; to impair; as, to prejudice a good cause.
quotes: Seek how may prejudice the foe.
